one_line_takeaway: By exchanging only cluster centroids of DCT-transformed, per-sample LoRA gradients between server and clients, ProtegoFed detects 92-100% of poisoned instruction-tuning samples and drives backdoor attack success rate to 0% across four FSQA datasets, four attack types, and nine LLM backbones, without needing to discard entire client updates -- addressing a 'malicious data provider inside benign clients' threat that prior client-level FL backdoor defenses (Krum, Median, Trimmed-mean, FreqFed, FoundationFL) and centralized sample-wise defenses (CUBE, GraCeFul, ONION) largely fail to handle once poisoned data is spread thinly across many clients.
key_finding: On the main IID benchmark (Table II/III, 4 datasets x 4 attacks = 16 settings), ProtegoFed reduces ASR to 0.00% in every single setting, versus baseline ASR of roughly 21-99% with no defense and still 6-99% residual ASR for the next-best defense (GraCeFul, the second-best baseline). CACC with ProtegoFed stays close to the no-defense baseline (e.g., beats the no-defense CACC in 9 of 16 settings) and clearly outperforms ONION, whose token-level edits destroy sentence content and crater CACC (e.g., WebQA CACC drops to ~8-10% under ONION). Detection quality (Table III): ProtegoFed achieves 92.00-100.00% recall across all 16 IID settings and reaches exactly 100.00% recall on FreebaseQA, CoQA and most WebQA/NQ cases, versus CUBE and ONION frequently scoring 0.00% recall (CUBE fails when per-client sample counts are small; ONION cannot strip multi-token or stylistic triggers) and GraCeFul degrading markedly under CBA (recall as low as ~31-77% in some rows). F1 for ProtegoFed reaches ~90-100% on FreebaseQA/CoQA/NQ, with a lower point of 79.12% on WebQA under CBA (recall there is still 99.08% -- the paper explicitly flags precision, not recall, as the weak point in that cell). Across 9 diverse model backbones (1B-70B params, Table IV), ProtegoFed drives ASR to 0.00% in every case while keeping recall >=94.80% (mostly 99.60-100%) with no consistent CACC degradation. On a fully clean dataset (Table V, no poisoning at all), ProtegoFed's false-positive filtering costs <1% CACC (e.g. 45.86% -> 45.28% on WebQA), i.e. it does not gut clean data when there is nothing to catch. Under skewed NIID-3 (worst case, upper poison-ratio cap removed), the second-best baseline's recall falls to about 50% while ProtegoFed still achieves near-perfect recall/ASR-elimination (per Table VII, recall >96% across alpha in {0.1,0.3,0.7,1.0}). An ablation (Table VIII) on a scenario where clean data dominates only 40% of clients (remaining 60% split into two 70%-poisoned groups with different triggers) shows the global secondary-clustering/local-revising mechanism is essential: without it, accuracy/precision/recall/F1 are 45.90/22.68/11.09/14.89%, and with it they jump to 99.29/99.58/98.76/99.17%.
core_contribution: ProtegoFed is a backdoor-free Federated Instruction Tuning (FIT) framework that detects, removes, and 'purifies' poisoned instruction-tuning samples that are interspersed across many otherwise-benign clients (a 'malicious user/data provider' threat rather than a 'malicious client' threat). It runs once, before FL training begins, in three phases: (1) Intra-Client frequency-based clustering -- each client computes sample-wise gradients on the lora_B parameters of the last transformer layer, applies a 2D discrete cosine transform (DCT) to get frequency-domain features, reduces dimensionality with UMAP, and clusters with an ensemble of hierarchical clustering and HDBSCAN (choosing whichever has the higher silhouette score, with a conservative silhouette-score threshold of 0.2/0.4 below which all data is treated as clean to avoid false positives); (2) Global secondary clustering -- the server collects each client's local main-cluster centroid (computed in the original, non-reduced frequency dimension so different clients' feature spaces are comparable), re-clusters these centroids, and computes a 'global centroid' from the largest cluster of centroids, discarding outlier clients whose local clustering was corrupted by heavy poisoning; (3) Local revising -- each client adds the broadcast global centroid as an extra point into its local feature set and re-clusters; the cluster containing the global centroid is deemed the clean cluster and used to filter the client's training data going forward. The paper also provides a convergence analysis for ProtegoFed (Theorem 1) by treating it as an instance of the same downsampling argument used for FedHDS (Qin et al.), and states the method is complementary to (and can be combined with) existing client-level Byzantine-robust aggregation defenses such as FreqFed.
threat_model: Adversary: a malicious end-user or third-party data provider who supplies poisoned question-answer training data (e.g., via LLM chat logs, crowdsourced datasets, or code-editor training-data channels) that gets inadvertently collected by benign FL clients/organizations -- NOT a malicious FL client and NOT a malicious server. The adversary has full ability to craft/modify the content of its own contributed samples but has no control over model training, local pre-processing, aggregation, or any other client's data. A 'certain proportion' of any given client's dataset may be poisoned, and in the worst case poisoned data can dominate some individual clients even though clean data dominates globally. Attack goal: implant a backdoor (trigger -> attacker-chosen malicious output, e.g. an injected malicious URL) while preserving normal task accuracy on clean inputs. Defender: all FL clients and the server are benign, cooperate voluntarily in the defense protocol, and the server does not require an auxiliary clean dataset. Defense objectives stated by the paper: prevent backdoor attacks, maintain model utility (CACC), preserve privacy (no raw data sharing -- only centroids are exchanged), and keep overhead low (a one-time pre-training step). The authors explicitly scope out attacker techniques for improving backdoor learnability (e.g., 3DFed) as orthogonal, since their threat model assumes no malicious clients; they study only the data-poisoning half of the attack pipeline.
limitations: The paper's own poison-ratio sweep (Fig. 6, Section VI-D2) shows ProtegoFed's defense explicitly breaks down when poisoned data dominates all clients simultaneously -- the authors state this is consistent with their design assumption that clean data must dominate on most clients for the global centroid to remain anchored in the clean-data region, and they argue (without a dedicated attacker-adaptivity experiment) that such an extreme, globally-dominant poison ratio would be impractical for a stealthy real-world attacker. The convergence proof (Theorem 1) is derivative, explicitly borrowed from the FedHDS downsampling proof (Qin et al.) rather than a new analysis tailored to ProtegoFed's clustering-based filtering. The threat model is deliberately narrow -- it assumes all clients and the server are benign and studies only the data-poisoning stage of a backdoor attack, explicitly treating techniques that make poisoned data easier to learn (e.g., 3DFed) as out of scope; a client-level attacker is not evaluated at all in the main results, only mentioned as combinable via existing defenses like FreqFed. Detection precision (not just recall) is uneven in the worst cells (F1 as low as 79.12% on WebQA/CBA), meaning some clean samples are still discarded even though poisoned samples are almost always caught. The method also depends on a single hand-picked target module (lora_B of the last transformer layer) and fixed silhouette-score thresholds (0.2/0.4) chosen as conservative defaults rather than tuned/justified per dataset.

datasets_used: Natural Questions (NQ) subset from Cheng et al. 2024 (TrojanRAG)
quantitative_results: ProtegoFed reduces ASR to 0.00% in all 16 IID dataset x attack combinations in Table II
ProtegoFed recall 92.00%-100.00% across all IID settings in Table III
ProtegoFed F1 reaches 79.12% (WebQA/CBA, worst case) up to ~100% (FreebaseQA/CoQA)
No-defense ASR ranges ~21.46% (WebQA/CBA) to 99.90% (FreebaseQA/StyleBkd) before any defense
GraCeFul (best baseline) leaves residual ASR from 0.00% to as high as 93.78% depending on dataset/attack
CUBE and ONION recall = 0.00% in most IID settings (fail to detect poisoned samples)
Across 9 model backbones (1B-70B params) ProtegoFed achieves ASR=0.00% and recall 94.80%-100.00% (Table IV)
On fully clean data, ProtegoFed costs <1% CACC (e.g., WebQA 45.86% -> 45.28%) (Table V)
NIID-3 (worst-case skew): second-best baseline recall drops to ~50%, ProtegoFed recall stays near-perfect (Table VII: recall >96% across alpha in {0.1,0.3,0.7,1.0})
Ablation on global secondary clustering (Table VIII): without revise, Accuracy/Precision/Recall/F1 = 45.90/22.68/11.09/14.89%; with revise = 99.29/99.58/98.76/99.17%
GraCeFul applied naively per-client (vs. centrally) drops to F1 21.25-21.74% (Llama) on WebQA IID with 10 clients, 10% poison ratio (Table I), motivating the need for ProtegoFed
